scores.dimension: D1 — Architectural coupling discipline (SC ↔ UI ↔ deploy-artefact)
scores.anchor_matched: 3 anchor: broadcast artifacts exist and the UI/docs are hand-wired; hard floor applies because registry/factory-era addresses disagree across UI and docs.

evidence.note: Primary registry address is an inline v5 string literal and the FutarchyRegistry ABI is hand-written in the UI.
Create flow duplicates the same inline registry address and ABI instead of loading a generated deployment manifest or binding.
Contracts page still labels and links a v3 registry address, conflicting with the v5 registry consumed by the JS.
Deployment manifest is markdown and lists the v0 stack by hand; several addresses differ from the active site-testnet contracts page.

scores.dimension: D2 — Security posture (admin keys, immutability, timelocks, multisig)
scores.anchor_matched: 0-3 band: AccessControl and owner roles exist, but testnet deployments use a single EOA admin with no Safe or Timelock; an operator key export appears in a runbook, which pulls the score below the 3 anchor.

evidence.note: DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE is granted directly to the constructor admin, and MINTER_ROLE gates minting.
Deploy script derives admin from PRIVATE_KEY and explicitly notes the EOA admin is only 'for now' / later Timelock.
Sepolia stack admin is vm.addr(deployerPK), i.e. the deployer EOA.
Orchestrator and evaluator owner/admin are wired to the EOA admin; no Safe or TimelockController is deployed in this script.
Admin can rotate DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE immediately from the current admin without an enforced timelock.
Runbook includes a concrete PRIVATE_KEY export pattern for the operator flow; the value is intentionally not reproduced here.

scores.dimension: D3 — Deprecation hygiene (v3 → v4 → v5 transitions)
scores.anchor_matched: 3 anchor, slightly below: version words and old deploy paths coexist, active UI/docs disagree, and a .todo-v3 test remains.

evidence.note: Visible site surface still presents the shared registry as v3 while the active JS consumes a v5 registry.
Current registry deploy script remains in the active script directory.
Older V3 registry deploy script remains beside the current script without an @deprecated marker or legacy/archive placement.
A .todo-v3 test file is still present in the main test tree.
Superseded v1 contracts are documented as left on-chain, but this is not represented in a machine-readable deprecated/replaced_by manifest.

scores.dimension: D4 — Operator surface readiness (daemons, runbooks, monitoring, alerting)
scores.anchor_matched: 3 anchor: daemons and collectors exist, but defaults drift, submit.py is still a scaffold, and there is no dry-run, heartbeat endpoint, alerting, or restart contract.

evidence.note: Header documents one set of default addresses and daemon settings.
Runtime config uses different factory/orchestrator/resolver/gas defaults than the header, showing header-vs-code drift.
needs_promotion is a stub that always returns None, so this daemon cannot yet promote candidates.
Polling collector has env defaults and output paths, but it is file/markdown oriented rather than a heartbeat metric plus HTTP/Prometheus endpoint.
Runbook checklist still leaves daemon runtime and adversarial agent execution unchecked; no rotate-operator-key or pause-sale runbooks are present.

scores.dimension: D5 — Maintainability and freshness (docs ↔ code; dead code; CI gating)
scores.anchor_matched: 3 anchor: docs are readable but stale paths and address drift remain; CI has Solidity and Slither checks but no UI/e2e, docs link check, manifest-vs-broadcast diff, or address-set gate.

evidence.note: README references registry.js and an old zero-address replacement workflow, but the current shared source of site state is shared.js and the UI no longer has that file.
Primary CI job runs forge fmt, forge build, and forge test only.
A Slither workflow exists and can fail on medium/high issues, but it is not a cross-layer docs/address/manifest freshness gate.
Wallet e2e journey is explicitly scaffolded as test.fixme and does not yet provide running cutover coverage.
A .todo-v3 test file remains in-tree, indicating unresolved legacy/deprecation cleanup.

scores.dimension: D6 — Supply-chain risk (npm pin, foundry submodule pin, RPC, CDN, verification)
scores.anchor_matched: 3 anchor: some Foundry/submodule pins exist, but npm ranges are floating, CDN scripts lack SRI, vendored dependency documentation is incomplete, RPCs are public single endpoints, and there is no repo SECURITY.md.

evidence.note: ethers is loaded from jsDelivr without an integrity attribute; the same pattern appears across the testnet pages.
Frontend/e2e dependencies use caret ranges and there is no lockfile in the repo root, so npm dependency resolution is not fully pinned.
Foundry dependencies are submodules by URL; commit pins are provided by git state/foundry.lock rather than documented per-vendor provenance.
forge-std, OpenZeppelin, RealityETH, and sx-evm have recorded tags/revisions, which is a positive pinning signal.
RealityETH has vendor provenance, but comparable repo-level VENDORED documentation is not present for the other vendored trees used by the stack.
Only a single Gnosis RPC endpoint is configured and Etherscan config is commented; no documented RPC failover or verification pipeline is present here.

weakest_dimension: D2 — Security posture (admin keys, immutability, timelocks, multisig)
evaluator_notes: No topic-1/topic-2/topic-3/topic-4 evaluation files were present under audit/evaluations, so dependency-cap inputs were assumed to be 5.0 per the topic-5 rubric and no dependency caps fired. No prior topic-5 JSONL line existed, so regression/improvement detection has no baseline. I did not query Etherscan live; D6 is based on repo-observable verification/supply-chain evidence. Pre-existing dirty file docs/phase5-report-live.md was left untouched.
